chat.author: Grace Rachmany
chat.text:
Jonathan Rosenberg said:

One big difference relative to SIP is the long-livedness potential here; that i might start an agent, communicate with it, leave it running, pick it up later from another place. SIP was designed for calls between two endpoints/devices.

This points at the idea that Agents need identities/identifiers that they carry with them across boundaries, which is one of the architectures we are advocating for. In that scenario, the "session" layer is much more lightweight in scope. It still seems like the session layer is needed in the short and medium term to address issues that are alive today with OAuth implementation. At the same time, how do we create specifications that don't end up blocking future "frameworks". My concern is that if the IETF moves too quickly on fixing a framework assumption, it might become irrelevant and block IETF from approving "redundant" protocols as they emerge as dominant.

Something to note from existing protocols: Take A2A  as an example, it already does have a primitive called,  context id that can be shared among agents who work on different tasks relevant to achieving the same objective/intent. Tasks are tagged with their own id  and similarly exchanged when necessary in the agent messages over http.
